questions.question: Define Binary Search Tree and state its time complexity for search, insert, and delete operations.
keyPoints: BST is a binary tree where left subtree has smaller values and right subtree has larger values
Time complexity is O(h) where h is height of tree
Best case O(log n) for balanced tree, worst case O(n) for skewed tree

questions.question: Explain the difference between best-case, worst-case, and average-case time complexity of BST operations.
keyPoints: Best case O(1) when element is at root
Average case O(log n) for randomly built BST
Worst case O(n) when tree is completely skewed
Average case depends on insertion order

questions.question: What is the height of a BST? How does it affect the time complexity of operations?
keyPoints: Height is the maximum number of edges from root to deepest leaf
All operations (search, insert, delete) take O(h) time
Balanced BST: h ≈ log n → O(log n) operations
Skewed BST: h = n-1 → O(n) operations

questions.question: Why does inserting elements in sorted order degrade BST performance?
keyPoints: Sorted insertion creates a completely skewed tree
Each new element becomes right child of previous (or left for descending order)
Tree becomes equivalent to linked list with height n
All operations become O(n) instead of O(log n)

questions.question: What is a self-balancing BST? Give examples and explain why they are used.
keyPoints: Self-balancing BST maintains O(log n) height automatically
Examples: AVL trees, Red-Black trees, B-trees
They use rotations and rebalancing after insertions/deletions
Ensures worst-case O(log n) for all operations

questions.question: Compare the time complexity of finding minimum and maximum elements in a BST.
keyPoints: Find minimum: traverse left children until leftmost node, O(h)
Find maximum: traverse right children until rightmost node, O(h)
Best case O(1) if root is min/max
In balanced BST: O(log n), in skewed: O(n)

questions.question: Explain why in-order traversal of BST takes O(n) time complexity.
keyPoints: In-order traversal visits every node exactly once
There are n nodes in the tree
Time complexity is Θ(n) regardless of tree shape
Cannot be improved by balancing since all nodes must be visited

questions.question: Derive the time complexity analysis for insertion in a BST. Discuss best, average, and worst cases with examples.
keyPoints: Insertion involves searching for appropriate position then adding node
Search phase: O(h) comparisons to find insertion point
Insertion at leaf: O(1) after finding position
Total: O(h) for all cases
Best case: O(log n) when balanced, Worst case: O(n) when skewed
Average case: O(log n) for random insertions

questions.question: Explain the deletion operation in BST. Discuss all three cases (node with no child, one child, two children) and their time complexities.
keyPoints: Case 1: No child (leaf) - simply remove, O(h)
Case 2: One child - replace node with its child, O(h)
Case 3: Two children - find in-order successor, replace, delete successor, O(h)
Finding successor/predecessor: O(h)
Overall deletion: O(h) in all cases
Worst case O(n) in skewed tree

questions.question: Compare Binary Search Tree with Array and Linked List for search operations. When should each be preferred?
keyPoints: Array: O(log n) binary search if sorted, O(n) if unsorted, O(1) access but expensive insert/delete
Linked List: O(n) for search always, O(1) insert/delete at head
BST: O(log n) average for search/insert/delete, O(1) access not possible
Use Array: when data is static, frequent random access needed
Use Linked List: when frequent insertions/deletions at ends, search not common
Use BST: when balanced, frequent search + modifications

questions.question: Design an algorithm to check if a BST is balanced. Analyze its time complexity. Can you optimize it?
keyPoints: A tree is balanced if height difference between left and right subtrees of every node ≤ 1
Naive approach: for each node compute heights of subtrees separately - O(n²)
Optimized approach: post-order traversal with height calculation - O(n)
During return from recursion, check balance and propagate height
If unbalanced at any node, can return error indicator
